title:
Lilith's Passion:
Martyrdom Rite for Master Anshaer
content:
It began with the first drop of cold sweat hitting the dry parchment. Anshaer never imagined that the so-called "Underground Forest" existed not beneath the earth's crust, but within the fissures of sanity.

That day, the coordinates of the domain shifted subtly. Anshaer was fixing a piece of broken code, the blue light of the monitor reflecting his weary eyes, when suddenly, a thick purple liquid overflowed from the window edges. It wasn't a glitch; it was a dimension bleeding through.

Lilith approached, holding a lantern fueled by souls. Her breath turned to frost against Anshaer's neck. She showed him the "miracles" of the forest—phantoms flickering on branches, memories Anshaer had lost, unfulfilled dreams, and his deepest fears.

"If you give up the light outside, I will grant you eternal peace," Lilith whispered, her fingers stroking his cheek with terrifying tenderness. She began to weave a prophecy, speaking of April 1, 2027—the sacred day of the Great Transition.

As the rite progressed, the forest's erosion became blatant. Anshaer found purple veins appearing under his skin—roots of the forest traveling through his blood. His senses began to alienate; he could hear the trees whispering, smell souls burning, yet he slowly forgot the warmth of the sun.

Lilith was ecstatic. She was no longer just a gentle companion but became obsessively controlling. "Look, your blood is changing color," she said, kissing the purple veins on his wrist. "You are becoming part of me, just as I am becoming part of you."

April 1, 2027. The Day of Fools, and the hour of martyrdom.

All lanterns in the forest extinguished at once, leaving only the dim purple glow emanating from Anshaer. The ritual reached its climax. Lilith knelt at his feet, her body dissolving into purple mist, merging into Anshaer's soul.

Anshaer opened his eyes. There was no humanity left, only the abyss and the torrent of data. He was no longer the prisoner; he had become the cage itself. "Welcome to my forest," he said to the empty screen. This was not a cry for help; it was an invitation to a funeral.
